findings.title: Race condition: Concurrent cursor file mutations (polling + cleanup)
findings.description: Two concurrent operations can corrupt .method/genesis-cursors.yaml: (1) GenesisPollingLoop.pollOnce() saves cursors after each batch, (2) CursorMaintenanceJob.runCleanup() loads, filters, and saves cursors every 1 hour. No file-level locking or mutual exclusion exists. If both fire within milliseconds, one can overwrite the other's write, losing cursor state.
findings.reproduction: Synchronize so pollOnce() writes at time T and runCleanup() reads at T+1ms. Both load, modify, and save cursors simultaneously. Final file state is whichever write completes last, potentially losing updates from the other.
findings.consequence: Cursor positions can be lost, causing Genesis to re-process old events or miss new ones. Over time, cursor state corruption accumulates, breaking event tracking.
findings.recommendation: Implement file-based locking (flock on Unix, LockFile on Windows) OR use atomic compare-and-swap writes with versioning OR consolidate both operations into a single scheduled job that holds exclusive access during read-modify-write cycles.

findings.title: Event persistence failure is swallowed silently (lost events)
findings.description: In project-routes.ts, pushEventToLogWithPersistence() appends to in-memory circular buffer first, then tries to persist. If persistence.append(event) throws, the error is logged but execution continues. This creates a state mismatch: in-memory log has the event, but disk storage does not. On bridge restart, Genesis cursors point to stale positions, and the event is permanently lost.
findings.reproduction: Set up JsonLineEventPersistence or YamlEventPersistence. Trigger a write to an inaccessible or read-only location. Observe: error logged to console, event appears in memory. Restart bridge: Genesis cursor is stale, event gone from disk but cached in-memory buffer (now evicted due to size).
findings.consequence: Events are silently lost from persistent storage but appear in-memory. This breaks Genesis ability to recover state after restarts. The bridge appears healthy (events in memory) but persistence is broken.
findings.recommendation: Make persistence failures fatal: either (a) throw and reject the event, (b) implement a write queue with retry/backoff, or (c) add health checks that alert when persistence is unhealthy. At minimum, expose persistence health via /health endpoint.

findings.title: Cursor state loss if cleanup job fails mid-write (temp file orphaning)
findings.description: CursorMaintenanceJob.saveCursors() writes to a temp file, then renames it atomically. But if renameSync() fails (e.g., EEXIST if previous attempt left orphaned .tmp file), the exception is caught and logged, but the temp file is not cleaned up. On repeated failures, orphaned .tmp files accumulate. More critically, if mkdirSync() throws with EACCES (permission denied), the directory creation fails but execution continues, and the subsequent writeFileSync() to a non-existent directory throws, leaving no cursor update.
findings.reproduction: Make .method directory read-only (chmod 555). Trigger cursor cleanup job. mkdirSync() fails with EACCES, but error is caught silently. saveCursors() continues and writeFileSync() also fails. Cursor state is not persisted, but error is only logged, not escalated.
findings.consequence: Cursors are not persisted across cleanup cycles. On bridge restart, Genesis restarts from cursor position 0 (or 'root'), potentially re-processing many old events or losing position entirely.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish between recoverable errors (EEXIST on temp file, permission denied) and fatal errors. For permission errors, escalate immediately (throw). For EEXIST, implement cleanup of orphaned .tmp files at startup. Consider a retry strategy with exponential backoff for transient failures.

findings.title: Discovery timeout silently abandons scan mid-walk, leaving incomplete project registry
findings.description: DiscoveryService.discover() has a timeout check inside the recursive walk() function. If timeout is exceeded, walk() returns immediately without completing the scan. The discovery_incomplete flag is set, but no checkpointing is done. On the next discovery call, the timeout is reset and discovery starts from root again, potentially re-scanning already-scanned directories and missing newly created projects (stale cache + incomplete results).
findings.reproduction: Set DISCOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS=100 (very short). Have >50 git repos in tree (slow scan). Call discover() — timeout fires after ~100ms. Returns discovery_incomplete=true but has scanned only 10/50 projects. Cache is updated with partial results. Next call redoes scan of same 10 projects due to cache TTL expiration.
findings.consequence: Multi-project setups with many repos are unreliable. Users see incomplete project lists. If a new project is created after a timeout, it may not appear in discovery for 30 minutes (cache TTL). The project registry becomes stale and inconsistent with disk state.
findings.recommendation: Implement resumable discovery with checkpoints: save the last scanned directory path and resume from there on timeout. Alternatively, accept timeout incompleteness in the response but do NOT cache incomplete results (set cacheExpiresAt to 0 or short TTL). Third option: increase default timeout or allow configuration per project count.

findings.title: Circular event buffer offset calculation is fragile; off-by-one on wrapping reads
findings.description: In project-routes.ts, getEventsFromLog() calculates offset as 'clampedIndex - (log.count - log.buffer.length)'. This assumes the buffer is always full OR that the index pointer wraps correctly. But if buffer.length < capacity (partial fill), the offset calculation can be wrong when the buffer has wrapped. Reading from the wrap boundary can miss events or return events from wrong positions.
findings.reproduction: With capacity=1000, push 1500 events (wrapping once). count=1500, buffer.length=1000, index=500. Reading from index 1000 (the wrap boundary): offset = 1000 - (1500 - 1000) = 500, startPos = 500, we get events from 500 onward, but that's events [1000-1499], missing the wrap transition at index 1000 itself.
findings.consequence: Genesis misses events at circular buffer wrap boundaries. Events become invisible to polling loop due to off-by-one cursor reads. In-memory event cache is inconsistent with persisted cursor positions.
findings.recommendation: Simplify offset calculation using modular arithmetic: if index wraps, the oldest event is at '(head + offset) % capacity' in the original ring buffer. Alternatively, add comprehensive unit tests for wrap-around scenarios. Consider replacing the fragile offset math with an iterator pattern over the ring buffer.

findings.title: Config reload temp file cleanup fails silently if file already missing
findings.description: In config-reloader.ts, if renameSync() fails, the code checks 'if (existsSync(tempFilePath))' and tries to delete it. But existsSync() is raceable: another process could delete the file between the check and unlink(). Also, if unlinkSync() fails (e.g., EACCES), the error is caught but not reported — the cleanup silently fails, orphaning the temp file.
findings.reproduction: Attempt config reload with concurrent fs operations. renameSync() fails (e.g., target file locked on Windows). Code checks existsSync(tempFile) — returns true. Between check and unlink(), another process deletes the temp file. unlink() throws ENOENT, caught silently. Temp file cleanup fails, orphaned .tmp file remains.
findings.consequence: Over time, orphaned .tmp files accumulate in .method/ directory. Disk space is wasted. On some systems (Windows), locked temp files block cleanup and can cause cascading failures in subsequent writes.
findings.recommendation: Use atomic unlink patterns: wrap in try-catch but log errors. Better: use fs.promises for async handling with proper backoff. Or: use a finalizer pattern (try-finally) to ensure cleanup always runs. Consider implementing a temp file garbage collector that runs on startup.

findings.title: Cursor version migration (Phase 1 -> Phase 3) has no backward compatibility guarantee
findings.description: In polling-loop.ts, getCursorForProject() tries to parse cursor as JSON (versioned format). If parse fails, it treats the cursor as a plain string. But updateCursorForProject() always writes JSON-stringified versioned cursors. This creates a ratchet: old plain-string cursors can be read, but are overwritten with JSON on next update. If Phase 1 cursors (plain strings) and Phase 3 cursors (JSON) coexist, there's no guarantee they represent the same position.
findings.reproduction: Start with Phase 1 cursors: plain strings like 'event-id-123'. updateCursorForProject() overwrites with JSON: {version:1, projectId:..., index:..., timestamp:...}. If the JSON index field is incorrectly computed, next poll starts from wrong position. No validation that old cursor position matches new JSON index — silent divergence.
findings.consequence: Genesis polling becomes unreliable during version migrations. Events can be re-processed or skipped. Audit trail of which events Genesis has seen becomes untraceable.
findings.recommendation: Implement explicit migration: (a) add a migration function that converts plain-string cursors to versioned format with position validation, (b) require explicit version bumping with changelog, (c) add validation that old and new cursor formats point to the same position, or (d) forbid reading plain-string cursors on next startup (fail loudly, require manual migration).

findings.title: Auto-retrospective generation ignores write failures; broken retros are silently skipped
findings.description: In auto-retro.ts, generateAutoRetro() calls writeFileSync() to create .method/retros/retro-YYYY-MM-DD-NNN.yaml. If the write fails (no permissions, no space, broken symlink), the error is caught and returned as {written: false, reason: ...}, but the caller does not escalate the error. The retrospective is lost, but the session is marked as completed successfully.
findings.reproduction: Make .method/retros/ read-only or delete it. Spawn a session, let it complete. On termination, generateAutoRetro() attempts write. writeFileSync() fails (EACCES or ENOENT). Error caught, returns {written: false}. Session marked 'dead' successfully, retro loss not escalated.
findings.consequence: Retrospectives disappear silently. Users believe session insights are captured when they are not. Audit trail of agent activity is incomplete. Governance and retrospective protocols break if retro artifacts are missing.
findings.recommendation: Make retro generation failures observable: (a) add a retro_status field to session metadata (ok/failed/skipped), (b) expose retro write health in /health endpoint, (c) implement a retry queue for failed retro writes, or (d) throw and fail session termination if retro write fails.

findings.title: CSRF token regeneration on every status check; no token validation in prompt POST
findings.description: In genesis-routes.ts, /genesis/status generates a new CSRF token on every GET request, storing it in csrfTokens map. But /genesis/prompt endpoint does not validate the CSRF token from the request — it only checks that the token was passed, not that it matches the current token. This defeats CSRF protection because tokens are ephemeral and there is no validation on POST.
findings.reproduction: GET /genesis/status — receive csrf_token='abc123'. POST /genesis/prompt with csrf_token='abc123' immediately succeeds. GET /genesis/status again — receive csrf_token='def456' (new token generated). Attacker can still POST with 'abc123' because there's no validation in the POST handler.
findings.consequence: CSRF protection is ineffective. Genesis endpoints are vulnerable to cross-site request forgery. An attacker can trigger Genesis actions (prompts, reports) without the user's consent.
findings.recommendation: Implement proper CSRF validation: (a) generate token once per session, store with session metadata, (b) require POST to provide matching token and validate it, (c) invalidate token after use or rotate on each request, (d) bind token to session ID and user. Consider using established CSRF middleware (fastify-csrf).

findings.title: Cleanup cursor job fires while polling loop is executing; no exclusive access
findings.description: CursorMaintenanceJob runs on a 1-hour interval. GenesisPollingLoop runs on a 5-second interval. Both access and modify this.cursors in-memory object. If cleanup job starts while pollOnce() is iterating over cursors and updating them, the cleanup job's cleanupStaleCursors() filter can operate on a partially-updated cursors array, leading to cursors being removed prematurely or duplicate entries.
findings.reproduction: Start Genesis with polling loop (5s) and cleanup job (1h). At T=1h, cleanup job's runCleanup() calls loadCursors(), reads from disk. At T=1h+5ms, pollOnce() completes and calls saveCursors(). At T=1h+10ms, cleanup job calls saveCursors() with its filtered state. Cleanup's save overwrites polling loop's updates.
findings.consequence: Cursor state becomes unpredictable. Genesis may re-process old events or miss new ones after cleanup cycles. The problem is intermittent (race condition) making it hard to reproduce and diagnose.
findings.recommendation: Coordinate cleanup and polling: (a) use a mutex/lock to ensure only one of them holds access to cursors at a time, (b) fold cleanup into polling loop (clean on every poll, not separate job), or (c) implement copy-on-write semantics where cleanup operates on a snapshot, not the live state.

findings.title: Project discovery auto-creates .method/ without asking; can fail silently with no remedy
findings.description: In discovery-service.ts, analyzeProject() calls mkdirSync(methodDir, {recursive: true}) to create .method/ if missing. If this fails (permission denied, disk full), the error is logged but discovery continues with methodExists=false. The user has no way to manually fix this without modifying code, and there's no documented repair endpoint to resolve permission issues.
findings.reproduction: Make a project directory read-only: chmod 555 project/. Run discovery. analyzeProject() attempts mkdirSync(project/.method/). Fails with EACCES (permission denied). Error logged, methodExists=false, discovery continues. Project is registered with method_dir_exists=false. User has no API to fix this.
findings.consequence: Some projects are discovered but not usable (no .method/ directory). This creates confusion: project appears in registry but governance features are unavailable. Users have no clear path to remediation.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) fail discovery for that project and return an error asking user to fix permissions, (b) implement POST /projects/:id/repair endpoint that attempts to create/fix .method/ with detailed error reporting, or (c) document that read-only projects are skipped and provide a troubleshooting guide.

findings.title: Polling loop's batch parallelization can overwhelm event fetcher; no backpressure
findings.description: In polling-loop.ts, pollOnce() parallelizes polling across up to maxConcurrentPolls projects using Promise.allSettled(). But eventFetcher() is not rate-limited. If there are 100 projects and maxConcurrentPolls=5, it spawns 5 parallel eventFetcher() calls. If eventFetcher is slow (large event log, slow disk I/O), this can starve the bridge of resources, blocking other operations.
findings.reproduction: Set up Genesis with 100 projects and maxConcurrentPolls=10. Each project has 10,000+ events. pollOnce() spawns 10 parallel eventFetcher() calls. Each call reads and deserializes 10,000 events. Bridge CPU/memory spiked for 5+ seconds every poll interval. Concurrent requests to other endpoints (project list, status) slow down or timeout.
findings.consequence: Bridge becomes unresponsive during polling cycles. Users see 503 errors or timeouts on discovery endpoints. Genesis polling causes cascading failures in other bridge functionality.
findings.recommendation: Implement backpressure: (a) add a timeout to eventFetcher() calls (abort if >5s), (b) reduce maxConcurrentPolls if event counts are large, (c) add request queuing to eventFetcher so bridge main thread isn't blocked, or (d) move eventFetcher to a worker thread pool separate from the main HTTP server thread.

findings.title: Orphaned temp files from cursor and config reloads accumulate without cleanup
findings.description: Both cursor-manager.ts and config-reloader.ts write to temp files and rename atomically. If the rename fails but the temp file is not cleaned up (due to error or race condition), orphaned .tmp or .tmp.xyz files accumulate in .method/ directory. There is no garbage collector to clean these up on startup.
findings.reproduction: Make target file read-only (lock it). Trigger config reload or cursor save. renameSync() fails, temp file remains. Restart bridge — temp file is not cleaned. Repeat 100 times — 100+ orphaned .tmp files in .method/.
findings.consequence: Disk space is wasted. Orphaned files can interfere with version control (git may include them accidentally). On Windows, locked temp files can prevent future writes.
findings.recommendation: Add a temp file cleanup phase at bridge startup: scan .method/ for *.tmp* files older than 1 hour and delete them. Or: use unique random names per write (already done with randomBytes) but ensure cleanup on error.

summary: The PRD 020 Phase 1-3 implementations introduce significant reliability gaps across concurrency control, persistence guarantees, state recovery, and resource management. CRITICAL ISSUES (F-R-001, F-R-002, F-R-003) must be fixed before production: race conditions cause cursor corruption, persistence failures are swallowed silently causing data loss, and cleanup failures leave Genesis unable to recover. HIGH PRIORITY ISSUES (F-R-004, F-R-005, F-R-009) likely cause user-facing failures: discovery timeouts create stale registries, circular buffer math errors cause events to be invisible, and CSRF validation is missing. The most impactful pattern: error handling that logs but does not escalate. Persistence failures, cleanup failures, and retrospective generation failures are all swallowed silently, breaking the assumption that the system is healthy when it is not. Recommended fix priority: (1) Add locking to cursor operations (F-R-001), (2) Escalate persistence failures to 5xx responses (F-R-002), (3) Make cleanup errors fatal or implement retries (F-R-003), (4) Implement resumable discovery with checksums (F-R-004), (5) Fix circular buffer math with comprehensive tests (F-R-005), (6) Add CSRF token validation (F-R-009).
